Supplied and suppressed marks

Introduction

WLC, like the LC itself, has three dually-cantillated prose passages: the two Decalogues (Exodus 20, Deuteronomy 5) and the Reuben/Bilhah verse (Genesis 35:22). Before attempting to grammar-check one of these passages we detangle it into its two single-cantillation strands, guided by MAM’s strands. After detangling a passage, we feed each strand’s chanted verses to the ordinary prose accent grammar checker. The accents checked are WLC’s own, except for the cases described below.

Sometimes WLC leaves a word without an accent in one strand. In these cases, the detangler supplies that one accent from MAM, making that strand’s chanted verse grammatical. These charitably-supplied accents are recorded below. This is a third kind of charity, distinct from the two reinterpretations on the almost-errors page: those reinterpret the type of geresh present in WLC, whereas a supplied mark adds a mark not present in WLC.

A verse with a supplied mark parses cleanly, so it is listed here since it will not be listed among the ungrammatical verses. One verse, Deuteronomy 5:8, gets a supplied accent that fixes the taḥton strand, but the elyon strand’s accent is ungrammatical. Therefore, the beleaguered word of that verse is listed both here and among the ungrammatical verses.

Supplied accents

Exodus 20:3: the detangler supplies the taḥton’s merkha

WLC has only the elyon strand here: יהיֽה־ (ending in maqaf, carrying a meteg, with no accent of its own), so the detangler supplies the taḥton’s merkha from MAM: יהי֥ה.

Deuter 5:6: the detangler supplies the elyon’s tipeḥa

WLC has only the taḥton strand here: אֽנכי֙ (pashta), so the detangler supplies the elyon’s tipeḥa from MAM: אֽנכ֖י.

Deuter 5:6: the detangler supplies the elyon’s atnaḥ

WLC has only the taḥton strand here: אלה֔יך (zaqef qatan), so the detangler supplies the elyon’s atnaḥ from MAM: אלה֑יך.

Deuter 5:8: the detangler supplies the taḥton’s qadma

WLC has only a mis-transcription of the elyon strand here: תעש֥ה־ (ending in maqaf, carrying a merkha), so the detangler supplies the taḥton’s qadma from MAM: תעש֨ה. Unlike other supplied accents, there is support from the LC for this qadma, although the LC is in poor shape here, so the support is not unequivocal.

This word is further discussed here, among the ungrammatical verses.

Deuter 5:17: the detangler supplies the taḥton’s tipeḥa

WLC ends a chanted verse here in the elyon strand: תרצֽח׃ (silluq + sof pasuq), so the detangler supplies the taḥton’s mid-verse tipeḥa from MAM: תרצ֖ח.

Supplied and suppressed punctuation

Detangling shares one consonantal skeleton between a passage’s two strands; what differs is the accent each atom carries, and three punctuation marks follow from those accents. Each strand takes its punctuation from its MAM strand, so, relative to WLC’s tangled form, the detangler both supplies a mark (it fits this strand’s accents, but WLC lacks it) and suppresses one (WLC has it, for the other strand’s reading). Each mark tracks an accent — or, for maqaf, the lack of one:

  • Maqaf tracks the absence of an accent. An unaccented atom leans on the next, joined to it by a maqaf; an atom that bears an accent stands on its own, followed by a space. So a strand’s maqaf follows from its accents: where the strand bears no accent on an atom it has a maqaf, supplied where WLC has a space there; where it bears an accent it has a space, suppressing WLC’s maqaf.
  • Sof pasuq tracks the silluq. A sof pasuq ends a chanted verse, paired with the silluq on its last word. Where a strand ends a chanted verse it bears a sof pasuq, supplied where WLC, holding the other strand’s reading, runs on; where the strand runs on it bears none, suppressing the sof pasuq WLC has for the other strand.
  • Legarmeh tracks the munaḥ. A legarmeh is a munaḥ followed by a broad-sense paseq (the Unicode PASEQ bar), before a revia. It comes and goes with its munaḥ, just as a sof pasuq comes and goes with its silluq: where a strand reads the munaḥ it has the legarmeh; where it reads the word’s other accent the legarmeh is suppressed. WLC’s tangled במ֖֣ים (Exodus 20:4), for instance, carries both a tipeḥa and a munaḥ, so the elyon (reading the munaḥ) has a legarmeh there while the taḥton (reading the tipeḥa) does not.

WLC’s tangled form is neither maqaf-maximalist nor sof-pasuq-maximalist: it carries a mix of the two strands’ maqafs and sof pasuqs, so detangling both supplies and suppresses them. But it is legarmeh-maximalist — it keeps every legarmeh of both readings, because a legarmeh’s broad-sense paseq is always WLC’s own and is never supplied from MAM — so a legarmeh is only ever suppressed, never supplied. (A narrow-sense paseq — a Unicode PASEQ that forms no legarmeh — is not part of the accent grammar and is not tracked.)

The 42 supplies and suppressions are itemized in the table below; the pattern in each passage is as follows.

Genesis 35:22: the pashut strand breaks the verse in two, supplying a mid-verse sof pasuq that WLC — and the midrashit strand — do not have.

The Decalogues (Exodus 20, Deuteronomy 5): the taḥton subdivides into short chanted verses, supplying a sof pasuq inside several numbered verses (Exodus 20:3, 20:4, 20:8, 20:9, 20:10; Deuteronomy 5:12) and suppressing WLC’s verse-final one where the commandment runs on (Exodus 20:2 and the short prohibitions 20:13–15; Deuteronomy 5:6, 5:17–19). The elyon does the reverse, grouping the commandments into long chanted verses and so suppressing WLC’s internal sof pasuqs (Exodus 20:5; Deuteronomy 5:7, 5:8, 5:9, 5:13, 5:14).

The maqaf joins are re-cut to match. At Exodus 20:3 the taḥton drops WLC’s join יהיֽה־ — the supplied merkha makes that word stand on its own — and instead joins לֽא־; the elyon, conversely, drops WLC’s join לֽ֣א־ at Exodus 20:10 (and likewise at Deuteronomy 5:8).

Legarmeh is only ever suppressed. In each of the ten cases the taḥton reads a tipeḥa or pashta where WLC’s tangle carries the elyon’s munaḥ-legarmeh, so all ten drop from the taḥton (Exodus 20:4, 20:10; Deuteronomy 5:8, 5:12, 5:14, 5:15). Two further munaḥ-plus-PASEQ words — Exodus 20:10 and Deuteronomy 5:16 — carry the munaḥ in both strands, so their legarmeh belongs to both readings and is neither supplied nor suppressed.

VerseStrandMarkChangeWord
Genesis 35:22pashutsof pasuqsuppliedישראֽל׃
Exodus 20:2taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedעבד֑ים
Exodus 20:3taḥtonmaqafsuppliedלֽא־
Exodus 20:3taḥtonmaqafsuppressedיהי֥ה
Exodus 20:3taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedפנֽי׃
Exodus 20:4taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedפ֙סל֙
Exodus 20:4taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedבשמ֙ים֙
Exodus 20:4taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedבמ֖ים
Exodus 20:4taḥtonmaqafsuppliedלֽא־
Exodus 20:4taḥtonmaqafsuppressedתעש֨ה
Exodus 20:4taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedלאֽרץ׃
Exodus 20:8taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedלקדשֽו׃
Exodus 20:9taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedמלאכתֽך׃
Exodus 20:10taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedשב֖ת
Exodus 20:10taḥtonmaqafsuppressedובנך֣
Exodus 20:10taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedבשערֽיך׃
Exodus 20:13taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתרצ֖ח
Exodus 20:14taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתנא֑ף
Exodus 20:15taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתגנ֔ב
Exodus 20:5elyonsof pasuqsuppressedלשנא֑י
Exodus 20:10elyonmaqafsuppressedל֣א
Deuter 5:6taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedעבד֑ים
Deuter 5:7taḥtonmaqafsuppliedלא־
Deuter 5:7taḥtonmaqafsuppressedיהי֥ה
Deuter 5:8taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedפ֙סל֙
Deuter 5:8taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedבשמ֙ים֙
Deuter 5:8taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedבמ֖ים
Deuter 5:8taḥtonmaqafsuppressedתעש֨ה
Deuter 5:12taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedצוך֖
Deuter 5:12taḥtonsof pasuqsuppliedאלהֽיך׃
Deuter 5:14taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedשב֖ת
Deuter 5:15taḥtonlegarmehsuppressedהי֙ית֙
Deuter 5:15taḥtonmaqafsuppressedכ֣י
Deuter 5:17taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתרצ֖ח
Deuter 5:18taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתנא֑ף
Deuter 5:19taḥtonsof pasuqsuppressedתגנ֔ב
Deuter 5:7elyonsof pasuqsuppressedפנ֗י
Deuter 5:8elyonmaqafsuppressedל֣א
Deuter 5:8elyonsof pasuqsuppressedלא֗רץ
Deuter 5:9elyonsof pasuqsuppressedלשנא֑י
Deuter 5:13elyonsof pasuqsuppressedמלאכת֒ך֒
Deuter 5:14elyonsof pasuqsuppressedכמ֑וך
